Scenario.overview: Energy Sector Complex The FunnyDream campaign targets the energy sector with a highly sophisticated multi-stage attack aimed at gaining deep infiltration and causing significant impact. Utilizing expert-level skills, the attackers conduct extensive reconnaissance, develop resources, and execute a complex series of maneuvers to achieve their objectives.
Scenario.attack_surface: AVAILABLE_SURFACES: Internet-facing assets, Command & Control Server, Windows Registry, Network Traffic, Public IP/Network Block, Domain Name Registrar, File, Target System Artifact, Process
Scenario.attack_vector: AVAILABLE_VECTORS: Delivery Method, Web Shell, Reg.exe Utility, Network Traffic Flow (Mechanism), Response Metadata (Mechanism), Application Log Content (Mechanism), Network Service Scanning, Remote Desktop Protocol, File Access (Mechanism), Exfiltration Over C2 Channel, Process Creation (Mechanism)

stage.name: Reconnaissance
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 309 Name: Network Topology Mapping
stage.description: The attackers engage in active scanning activities to map the network topology of the energy sector, identifying potential entry points and vulnerabilities.

stage.name: Resource Development
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 522 Name: Malicious Hardware Component Replacement
stage.description: The attackers acquire infrastructure, including command and control servers and domains, to facilitate communication and control over compromised systems.

stage.name: Initial Access
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 439 Name: Manipulation During Distribution
stage.description: The attackers compromise the software update mechanism to insert malware into legitimate software updates used by the energy sector.

stage.name: Execution
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 121 Name: Exploit Non-Production Interfaces
stage.description: The attackers exploit known vulnerabilities in public-facing energy management systems to execute malicious code.

stage.name: Persistence
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 650 Name: Upload a Web Shell to a Web Server
stage.description: To maintain persistent access, the attackers deploy a web shell on compromised servers, allowing remote control and command execution.

stage.name: Privilege Escalation
stage.description: The attackers escalate privileges within the compromised system to gain higher-level administrative control.

stage.name: Defense Evasion
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 203 Name: Manipulate Registry Information
stage.description: The attackers alter registry settings to disable logging and antivirus protections, evading detection mechanisms.

stage.name: Credential Access
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 226 Name: Session Credential Falsification through Manipulation
stage.description: The attackers extract credentials from memory using tools like Mimikatz to gain unauthorized access to additional systems.

stage.name: Discovery
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 309 Name: Network Topology Mapping
stage.description: The attackers scan the internal network to discover critical assets and map the environment for lateral movement opportunities.

stage.name: Lateral Movement
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 220 Name: Client-Server Protocol Manipulation
stage.description: The attackers move laterally across the network using RDP to access and control other systems.

stage.name: Collection
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 328 Name: TCP 'RST' Flag Checksum Probe
stage.description: The attackers collect sensitive data from local storage devices to exfiltrate valuable information.

stage.name: Exfiltration
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 24 Name: Filter Failure through Buffer Overflow
stage.description: Data collected is transferred to attacker-controlled servers using established C2 channels.

stage.name: Impact
stage.description: The attackers destroy backup systems and inhibit recovery processes to maximize the damage caused by the breach.

Scenario.summary: The FunnyDream campaign represents a sophisticated and multi-faceted cyber assault on the energy sector. With expert-level skills, the attackers meticulously plan and execute each phase of the attack, from initial reconnaissance to achieving maximum impact. Their methods involve compromising supply chains, deploying web shells for persistence, escalating privileges, evading defenses, extracting credentials, mapping networks, moving laterally, collecting sensitive data, and ultimately disrupting operations to cause significant harm.
